coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of ZnO nanoparticles-based fluorescent ink for information encryption and security applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Nguyen, Thanh Phuong
Dang, Huu Phuc
Nguyen, Long Giang
Le, Cong Danh
coredata.dc:description: Luminescent ZnO nano pigments with a hexagonal structure were successfully synthesized using the hydrothermal method. The color fluorescent emission of ink can be tuned by hydrothermal conditions in the synthesis of pigment or by adjusting the pH values of the ink formulation. The ink formula based on luminescent ZnO nano pigments with a concentration of 15 % (w/w) was dispersed and homogenized in the vehicle, including water, PVA, and ethylene glycol. The stable ZnO nano ink formula has the zeta potential value of ξ = −24.8 mV at pH = 7.2, and ZnO pigments have an average particle size of about 500 nm in length and 90 nm in width. The logo samples printed on graphic paper and Couche paper with luminescent ink using a screen printing method show a bright yellow fluorescence at 565 nm under UV irradiation 365 nm and are invisible under normal light. The QR code on paper is readable by a smartphone under UV light. The ZnO nano ink is suitable for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO nanoparticles
Luminescence printing
Counterfeiting
Luminescent ink
Encryption

Optical results of ZnO nano pigments (ZnO NPs)
Structural and morphological results of ZnO nano ink
Optical results of ZnO nano ink
Zeta potential (ξ) and size distribution of ZnO nano ink
Applications of ZnO nano ink for anti-counterfeiting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of ZnO nano-pigment and (b) Reparation of ZnO nano-pigment inks.
(a) XRD patterns for ZnO nanoparticles synthesized in an autoclave at different temperatures, (b) Williamson-Hall plots of ZnO nanoparticles with varying temperatures.
SEM images of ZnO powder with varying temperatures.

(a) PL spectra for ZnO nanoparticles with varying temperatures, (b) Schematic illustration of the emission processes of ZnO nanoparticles, (c) CIE color coordinate of ZnO nanoparticles with varying temperatures.
(a) XRD patterns for ZnO nanoparticles and ZnO nano ink, (b) SEM image of ZnO nano ink.
FT-IR spectra of inks with different pH values, ZnO nano-pigment, and PVA solution.
(a) PL spectra for ZnO nanoparticles fluorescent inks with pH values of 5.2, 7.2, and 10.2, (b) CIE color coordinate of the fluorescent inks with pH values of 5.2, 7.2, and 10.2.
(a) Zeta potential ZnO nanoparticles (100 mg/l), (b) Size distribution of ZnO nanoparticles (100 mg/l).

(a) ZnO nanoparticles fluorescent inks with different pH values under sunlight and UV 365 nm light, (b) The logo of the Graphic Arts and Media Faculty and HCMUTE printed by screen printing method, (c) The fluorescent inks with different pH values printed on graphic paper under normal light and UV 365 nm light.

(a) PL spectra for ZnO nanoparticles fluorescent inks with the pH values of 7.2, (b) CIE (x, y) color coordinate of the fluorescent inks, (c) Schematic diagram of encryption QR code printed by screen printing on graphic paper and decryption QR code using mobile phone.
Structural parameters for ZnO nano pigments with varying temperatures.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of ZnO nanoparticles-based fluorescent ink for information encryption and security applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Printing Material Lab, Faculty of Graphic Arts and Media, HCMC University of Technology and Education, No. 1 Vo Van Ngan Street, Linh Chieu Ward, Thu Duc District, Ho Chi Minh City, 70000, Viet Nam

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Graphic Arts and Media
HCMC University of Technology and Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Printing Material Lab, Faculty of Graphic Arts and Media, HCMC University of Technology and Education, No. 1 Vo Van Ngan Street, Linh Chieu Ward, Thu Duc District, Ho Chi Minh City, 70000, Vietnam

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Fundamental Science, Industrial University of Ho Chi Minh City, No. 12 Nguyen Van Bao Street, Ward 4, Go Vap District, Ho Chi Minh City, 70000, Viet Nam

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Industrial University of Ho Chi Minh City

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Fundamental Science, Industrial University of Ho Chi Minh City, No. 12 Nguyen Van Bao Street, Ward 4, Go Vap District, Ho Chi Minh City 70000, Vietnam

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Luminescent ZnO nano pigments with a hexagonal structure were successfully synthesized using the hydrothermal method. The color fluorescent emission of ink can be tuned by hydrothermal conditions in the synthesis of pigment or by adjusting the pH values of the ink formulation. The ink formula based on luminescent ZnO nano pigments with a concentration of 15 % (w/w) was dispersed and homogenized in the vehicle, including water, PVA, and ethylene glycol. The stable ZnO nano ink formula has the zeta potential value of ξ = −24.8 mV at pH = 7.2, and ZnO pigments have an average particle size of about 500 nm in length and 90 nm in width. The logo samples printed on graphic paper and Couche paper with luminescent ink using a screen printing method show a bright yellow fluorescence at 565 nm under UV irradiation 365 nm and are invisible under normal light. The QR code on paper is readable by a smartphone under UV light. The ZnO nano ink is suitable for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As we know, counterfeiting is a widespread, serious problem that could negatively impact the growth of the economy through the circulation of illegal currency, goods, documents, or medicinal products. As a result, anti-counterfeiting technologies become a hot topic and attract many researchers [1–4]. In addition, security printing also employs various techniques and technologies to deter counterfeiting related to information encryption [3,5,6], security packaging [7], smart labels [8], anti-counterfeiting printing ink [9,10], etc. Among these technologies, luminescence printing based on luminescent materials is widely used against counterfeiting because of several advantages, such as the simple design of luminescent inks and the many different colors [11].

Toward the development of these technologies, many different new luminescent materials have been studied and developed, including bithiophene-based chalcone (BTCF) [12], thiophene-based OB [2,5-bis-(5-tert-butyl-benzoxazol-2-yl)-thiophene (BBOT)] [13], carbon dots [14–16], rare-earth nano phosphor [17], and quantum dots (QD) [9]. However, the selection of appropriate materials is essential to developing fluorescent ink. For example, these materials have good yields and are cost-effective, sustainable environment, and non-toxic. Among luminescent semiconductor nanomaterials, such as CdS, SdSe, CdTe, and ZnS, ZnO nanoparticles (ZnO NPs) are non-toxicity, eco-friendly semiconductors and high stability with a large exciton binding energy (≈60 eV at room temperature). Moreover, ZnO is also considered an essential additive in food and drugs by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [18]. These previous studies have shown that ZnO NPs are applied in optoelectronics [19], photocatalysis [20], solar cells [21,22], and gas sensors [23]. ZnO nanomaterials are also widely applied in packaging and printing technologies. For instance, ZnO nanoparticles were used in food packaging in combination with chitosan/gelatin, ZnO NPs dispersed in poly(vinyl alcohol) films or ZnO-doped carbon nanotubes dispersed poly(vinyl alcohol) [24,25]. ZnO additives have also been applied for antibacterial activities in conventional ink [26] or photo-curing of UV offset printing inks to enhance faster drying of ultraviolet ink [27,28]. For information security and security printing, ZnO NPs have strong fluorescent emissions in visible light [5,9,10], making them a promising candidate for anti-counterfeiting printing ink. In general, security inks refer to inks that are designed to prevent counterfeiting and unauthorized reproduction of documents or products.

Chong-Xin Shan et al. [6] reported that ZnO NPs were coated on paper, and the CuCl2 solution was used as ink, which is visible under UV illumination. The study results show a method for advanced encryption. Then, P. Swaminathan and co-workers [29] applied ZnO nanoparticles for inkjet printing, and the result indicates that the ink has a potential for printed oxide electronic devices. Recently, Qi Wang and co-workers [9] reported that ZnO quantum dots were used as fluorescent pigments in anti-counterfeiting ink applications. The study results show that the ink emits yellow fluorescence at about 536 nm and 617 nm under UV illumination. Besides, U. Ngoensawat et al. [10] have also demonstrated that ZnO quantum dots/bacterial cellulose nanocrystal (BCNC) nanohybrid have great potential as security marking ink for counterfeit protection. Very recently, M. R. Chandana et al. [30] reported that ZnO nanoparticles were used as the fluorescent pigment in the water-based fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink. In this study, water was used as the solvent, and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was employed as the viscosity modifier. The result indicated that the ink emitted a strong blue fluorescence at a wavelength of about 400–460 nm under UV 365 excitation. In addition, previous studies show that PVA has also been used as a binder to keep nanoparticles as carbon dots (CDs) [31], Gd2O3:Eu3+ [32], graphene oxide nanocomposites [33], CuO nanoparticles [34] due to favorable properties of PVA, such as non-toxicity, flexible, excellent film forming, and high transmittance over the whole visible light. Moreover, PVA's abundant hydroxyl groups (-OH) favor cross-linking with doping nanoparticles [35].

The above analysis results show that almost all research groups point out the potential application of ZnO nanoparticles in security inks and have not yet investigated the characteristics of inks in detail. As a result, we have systematically studied the intrinsic properties of the ink and employed ZnO nanoparticle-based fluorescent ink for information encryption and security applications. In this study, we have focused on essential ink properties, such as eco-friendly vehicles, optimal pH values for luminescent ink, and the performance and stability of ZnO nano ink.

Zinc acetate (Zn(CH3COO)2.2H2O, 99 %), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH 96 %), and Poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) used as a distribution media and the viscosity modifier of ink were purchased from Aldrich. Citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7.H2O) and ethylene glycol (C2H6O2) were purchased from Xilong.

In this paper, ZnO nano pigments were synthesized using the hydrothermal method, as shown in Fig. 1 (a). The first solution (0.2 M), containing 4.4 g of Zn(CH3COO)2.2H2O, was dissolved in 100 ml of deionized water for 30 min at 80 °C. Then, 2.9 g of NaOH (1.4 M) was dissolved in 50 ml of deionized water for 30 min at 80 °C. The mixture of the first and second solutions was continuously stirred for 30 min at 80 °C to get the colloidal solution. The solution was then placed in the Teflon stainless steel autoclave, which was hydrothermally heated at 100 °C for 12 h and then cooled to room temperature. A similar procedure was performed, and the colloidal solutions were placed in the autoclave for 12 h at 150 °C, 200 °C and 250 °C, respectively. The wet precipitates were washed several times with distilled water and ethanol. Finally, ZnO nano pigments synthesized at these different temperatures were collected and dried in air at 80 °C for 24h, as illustrated in Fig. 1(a).

In this study, poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) was used as a distribution media and the viscosity modifier. To synthesize ZnO nanopigment ink, 10 g of PVA was dissolved in 100 ml of deionized water using a magnetic stirrer. Then, 10 g of Ethylene Glycol (EG) was added to the solution, and the mixture was continuously stirred for 30 min at 60 °C to get a transparent vehicle solution. Lately, 15 % (w/w) ZnO nano pigment was dispersed in the vehicle, including water, PVA, and EG, to obtain an ink formulation. The ink was vigorously stirred for 1 h and homogenized using the ultrasonic method for 30 min. Fig. 1(b) displays a schematic diagram of the preparation for the ink formula of luminescent ZnO nano pigments. In this study, citric acid was used to adjust the pH of the ink, which was adjusted to 5.2, 7.2, 9.2, and 10.2. The ZnO ink was printed on graphic and Couche papers using the screen printing technique.

XRD patterns of ZnO nano pigments and inks were recorded using a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer (Cu Kα, λ = 1.54 Å). The JEOL JSM-7600 F model was used to investigate the surface morphology of the pigments. UV–Vis spectrum was recorded on a Jasco V-670 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrometer was used to record the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of ZnO nano pigments. FT-IR spectra were performed on the Shimadzu spectrophotometer. A Zetasizer Nano ZS (UK) was used to analyze zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement. The Q500 Sonicator was used to disperse ZnO nano pigments. The screen printing mold was fabricated on Screen Meister MDS 360.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological results of ZnO nano pigments (NPs)
ce:section.ce:para: The crystal quality of pigment affects the performance of ink, and the higher the purity of pigment, the better the ink quality. Fig. 2 (a) shows XRD patterns for ZnO nano pigments synthesized in an autoclave at different temperatures. The investigated results indicated that the planes of the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112), and (201) are assigned to the hexagonal structure (JCPDS No. 01-080-0074) [36]. The results indicate that the pure ZnO nano pigments were successfully synthesized using the hydrothermal method. In addition, the plane of (101) corresponding to 2θ = 36.5° has the highest intensity, indicating the dominant crystal growth. The result reveals that the crystallinity of ZnO NPs decreases with the autoclave reaction temperature increase, and it also agrees with the investigation result of Ref. [37].

In addition, the significance of the broadening of ZnO nano pigments diffraction peaks is also due to a linear combination of the contribution from small grain size and local strain in the crystal structure from defects [38,39]. Fig. 2(b) displays Williamson – Hall plots of ZnO nano pigment synthesized with varying autoclave reaction temperatures (100, 150, 200, 250, and 300oC). The micro-strain (ε) due to crystal imperfection and distortion of the ZnO nano pigments was estimated by equation (1) [39].
(1)
β
h k l
cos
θ =
0.9 λ
D
+ 4 ε
sin
θ
Where β hkl is the full width half maximum (FWHM) value at 2θ angles (the Bragg angle) corresponding to the hkl planes in radians, D is the average crystallite sizes, and ε is the micro-strain values of ZnO nano pigments.

The average crystallite sizes (D) and the micro-strain (ε) of ZnO NPs calculated from formula (1) [38,39] are shown in Table 1 . The D values are about 28.23, 28.03, 16.96, 15.37, and 14.98 nm for 100, 150, 200, 250, and 300 °C, respectively. The average crystalline sizes decrease with increased temperatures during the hydrothermal process. The result is due to the partial dissolution of ZnO NPs at high pressure under hydrothermal conditions [40]. However, M. M. ElFaham et al. [41] reported that particle sizes decrease as the hydrothermal reaction temperature increases. The result is explained by a shift of absorption peaks toward a longer wavelength, leading to a decrease in particle size. Generally, the crystalline sizes and micro-strain can be affected by factors such as annealing, defects and grain boundaries, phase transitions, quantum confinement, and size effects. The micro-tension strain values of ZnO NPs in Table 1 decrease with the increase in temperature in the hydrothermal process. The results show that the strain values increase with decreasing the average crystallite size. The result is suitable with the report of the authors in Ref. [42]. In addition, V. D. Mote and co-workers [42] reported that the c/a ratio increase might be affected by higher strain. The XRD analysis result also indicates that temperature can influence the mobility of defects and the migration of grain boundaries in ZnO nanoparticles. This movement can lead to strain relaxation as defects rearrange or grain boundaries adjust [43].

The morphology of ZnO particles plays a crucial role in determining their properties and applications. Therefore, controlling and understanding the factors influencing the morphology during synthesis is essential for designing ZnO particles with desired characteristics for specific applications. Fig. 3 shows SEM images of ZnO particles synthesized in an autoclave at temperatures of 100oC, 150oC, 200oC and 300oC. The analysis results show that ZnO micro-particles have rod-shaped morphology. At 100oC, the average length and width of ZnO particles are about 3.56 μm and 0.67 μm, respectively. From 150oC to 300oC, the average particle sizes are from 2.28 to 2.37 μm and 0.43–0.64 μm, corresponding to the length and width of ZnO particles. This result agrees with the XRD analysis and the study of Ref. [40]. In addition, C. H. Lu and co-workers [40] reported that the yield of ZnO powder decreases with the increase of the reaction temperature, and the reduction of particle sizes may be due to the partial dissolution of ZnO powder under high pressure under hydrothermal conditions. The average particle sizes are micro-particles due to the resolution of SEM equipment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical results of ZnO nano pigments (ZnO NPs)
ce:section.ce:para: ZnO NPs exhibit fascinating optical properties due to their unique crystal structure and size-dependent quantum confinement effects [44,45]. The photoluminescence (PL) properties of ZnO NPs depend on various factors such as size, shape, surface defects, and doping [46,47]. Fig. 4 (a) reveals photoluminescence (PL) spectra of ZnO nano pigment fabricated at varying temperatures under an excitation wavelength of 325 nm recorded at room temperature. The PL results indicate that ZnO NPs typically show two main emission peaks: a near-band-edge emission at about 385 nm related to excitonic transitions and a broad deep-level emission at about 565 nm attributed to defects in the ZnO lattice such as oxygen vacancies (VO), zinc vacancy (VZn), oxygen interstitial (Oi), and zinc interstitial (Zni) [47–49]. The emission processes of ZnO nanoparticles are illustrated in Fig. 4(b). The strong yellow emission observed at 565 nm is due to the electronic recombination from VO to VZn states, while a green emission at the wavelength of 500–555 nm is attributed to the recombination between the VO states and the valence band [44] (Fi. 4(b). The sharp emission peak at 650 nm indicates the transition between Zni and Oi states [44,49,50]. The near-infrared (NIR) emission that emerged at 750 nm is attributed to the transition from VO to VZn states. Besides, a small blue emission at about 425–450 nm originates from recombination from Zni states to the valence band [44,49]. However, the PL analysis results show that the PL intensity related to the near-band-edge emission and these defects in the broad deep-level emission decreases with increased synthesized temperature from 100 to 300 °C, respectively. The result is that the decrease in crystallinity affects the intensity and position of the emission peaks. It also agrees with the XRD analysis and the study result of Ref. [37]. Furthermore, under high temperatures in a hydrothermal process, the yield of ZnO powder decreases due to the partial dissolution of ZnO NPs [40]. Besides, the emission intensity at 650 nm related to the recombination between Zni and Oi states is enhanced, possibly due to the formation of oxygen interstitial (Oi) states with increased synthesized temperature.

Fig. 4(c) reveals the CIE (Commission Internationale de l'éclairage) color coordinate of ZnO NPs with varying temperatures. At 100oC, PL emission is yellow with the CIE (x, y) of (0.43, 0.47), and the color PL emission shifts to red with temperature increase from 150oC to 300oC. The fluorescence emission color change shows that ZnO nanoparticles have potential in security ink applications with different PL emissions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological results of ZnO nano ink
ce:section.ce:para: ZnO nano ink is an ink formulation that incorporates ZnO nano pigments as a critical component. This ink can be used for security features due to the unique properties of ZnO nanoparticles. In this paper, we chose ZnO nano pigments synthesized at 100 °C for security ink formulation. Fig. 5 (a) displays XRD patterns for ZnO nano pigments and ink formulation based on ZnO nano pigment. The result indicates that ZnO nano pigments with the planes assigned of (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), and (112) were successfully dispersed in the PVA solution. Besides, the X-ray diffraction peaks that emerged at about 2θ = 23.9°, 27.2°, and 43.1° are attributed to the PVA molecule's semi-crystalline nature [51,52]. The SEM image analysis in Fig. 5(b) shows that ZnO nano pigments have a particle size of about 500 nm in length and about 90 nm in width. The average particle size is smaller than that of the SEM analysis of ZnO powder because of the ultrasonic method for ink formulation. Thus, the result analysis in Fig. 5 indicates that the ZnO nano ink has been successfully synthesized in this paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical results of ZnO nano ink
ce:section.ce:para: The Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy can provide valuable information about the functional groups on the ZnO nanoparticle surface and their interactions with surrounding PVA molecules. Fig. 6 shows FT-IR spectra of ZnO nano pigments, PVA solution, and ZnO nano ink with different pH values. The prominent peak observed in the 462 to 670 cm−1 is typically attributed to the stretching vibrations of Zn–O bonds [53]. Moreover, the shift in the spectral peaks of ZnO NPs compared to the ZnO nano ink attributed to the interaction between OH groups of the PVA solution and Zn2+ ions [44,51]. The result is indicative of the presence of ZnO NPs in ink formulation. Besides, the vibration of groups of PVA molecules was also observed at 835–875 cm−1 of –CH2 stretching vibration [44]; 1035–1080 cm−1 of C–O stretching vibration from amorphous regions [51]; 1325–1415 cm−1 of –CH2 wagging [52]; 1645 cm−1 of O–H stretching and deformation vibration in the PVA surface [54]; 2905–2950 cm−1of C–H stretching vibration [53]. Stretching vibrations of O–H groups were observed at 3270–3397 cm−1, indicating the presence of water molecules in all samples.

As we know, in addition to various factors such as size, shape, surface defects, and doping [46,47], the pH value can also significantly impact the properties of ink, particularly in the photoluminescence performance of ZnO nano pigments. The authors of Ref. [6,9,55,56] reported that when the pH values are below 5.5, the ZnO solutions have no visible fluorescence under UV excitation. The result explains that the ZnO NP solution does not have absorbability in the ultraviolet region at pH < 5, and the pH value for photoluminescence is 6 (pH = 6) [9]. Moreover, ZnO NPs were decomposed in an acidic environment; thus, the photoluminescence quenching occurs at pH < 5 [6]. In this study, we synthesized ZnO nano inks with different pH values. Fig. 7 (a) displays PL spectra of ZnO nanoparticles fluorescent inks with pH values of 5.2, 7.2, and 10.2. At the pH value of 7.2, the PL intensities of ZnO nanoparticles related to broad defect-related emission peaks at 565 and 650 nm are the highest. At higher pH values (pH = 10.2), the PL intensities of these peaks are reduced due to the agglomeration of ZnO NPs [9]. At pH = 5.2, there is no photoluminescence under UV excitation, which is suitable with the result of Ref. [9]. These pH-induced changes in surface charge can affect the formation and stability of surface defects such as oxygen vacancies or other defects [6,57], thereby influencing photoluminescence properties. Besides, Fig. 7(b) shows the inks’ CIE (x, y) color coordinates, respectively. The CIE (x, y) coordinates also change from yellow (0.42, 0.46) to near white (0.32, 0.35). The PL analysis results in this study show that the pH values affect the photoluminescence intensity and CIE (x, y) color coordinate of ZnO nanoparticles, and the pH = 7.2 is very suitable for applications of security ink.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zeta potential (ξ) and size distribution of ZnO nano ink
ce:section.ce:para: Both zeta potential (charged surface) and size distribution parameters are crucial parameters for optimizing the performance and stability of ZnO nano ink. A high absolute zeta potential value (positive or negative) indicates good dispersion stability due to electrostatic repulsion between particles. Fig. 8 (a) shows the zeta potential of ZnO nanoparticles in water medium (100 mg/l). P. Swaminathan and co-workers [29] reported that water-based ZnO ink was more stable than inks based on ethylene glycol. The zeta potential value is about ξ = −24.8 mV at pH = 7.2, and the ξ value is a negative charge because of lattice defects [58]. The ξ = −24.8 mV indicates stable ZnO nano inks with a low possibility of aggregation [53,59]. ZnO nano pigments with a greater ξ value show a higher ink formula with high stability because of the greater electrostatic repulsion among them. Furthermore, Omar et al. [60] reported that the zeta potential of ZnO NPs is about +23 mV (at pH = 7.3 and 7.7) and unaffected by the concentration of ZnO NPs. The positive ξ value is due to adding cationic surfactants. Besides, the size distribution of ZnO nano pigments was recorded using the dynamic light scattering (DLS) method, which is shown in Fig. 8(b). The result in Fig. 8(b) reveals that the size distribution of ZnO NPs is about 278 nm with the highest frequency. This value may be suitable with the SEM image analysis in Fig. 5(b) because the DLS method yields one maximum in length and width [60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of ZnO nano ink for anti-counterfeiting
ce:section.ce:para: Anti-counterfeiting inks based on various materials [12,16,17,61,62] are designed to prevent counterfeiting and unauthorized reproduction of products. Fig. 9 (a) shows ZnO fluorescent inks with different pH values (pH = 5.2, 7.2, 9.2, and 10.2) under normal light and the UV 365 nm light excitation condition. The ink formulations with pH = 7.2, 9.2, and 10.2 show a bright yellow fluorescence under UV irradiation 365 nm, while the ink with pH = 5.2 has no emission under the UV 365 nm excitation. The results have been explained in detail in Section 3.2. As a result, these inks with pH = 5.2, 7.2, and 10.2 were used to print on graphic papers by a screen printing method. Fig. 9(b) displays the logos of the “Faculty of Graphic Arts and Media and HCMUTE” printed on paper by the screen printing method. These logos printed with ink (pH = 7.2) are invisible under normal light and have a strong yellow emission under the UV 365 nm light. The samples printed with inks (pH = 10.2) display a weaker yellow emission when exposed to UV 365 nm light (Fig. 9(c)). The results agree with the PL analysis in Fig. 7(a), and it is suitable for applications in documents, currency, or packaging to prevent counterfeiting. In addition, the print contrast of the logos under UV 365 nm light of the ink with pH = 7.2 is higher than that of pH = 10.2. Fig. 10 (a) shows UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra of the ink with pH = 7.2. The UV–vis spectrum shows the ink's absorption peak at 360 nm. The result indicates that the luminescent ZnO ink is very suitable for UV light source excitation with a wavelength of 365 nm. The PL spectra and the inserted photographs show that the ink has a bright yellow emission under the UV excitation, and the CIE (x, y) coordinates are respectively displayed in Fig. 10 (b). Fig. 10(c) shows that the QR code was printed on Couche paper by a screen printing method. The QR code is invisible under normal light and appears under a UV 365 nm light source. The blue background under UV light is due to the optical brightening agent (OBA) used in the paper. As a result, the yellow emission of the ZnO fluorescent ink combined with the blue emission of OBA was merged under the UV light excitation. Furthermore, we see that the QR code is readable by using a smartphone under UV light, and the data decryption is identified in the text “Printing Materials LAB” address. The results of this study not only fully reflect the optical, structural, and anti-counterfeiting characteristics but also combine printing technology to improve information security.

In this study, ZnO nanoparticles were successfully synthesized by the hydrothermal method at the hydrothermal reaction temperatures of 100, 150, 200, 250, and 300oC. ZnO nanoparticles with a hexagonal structure and rod-shaped morphology were dispersed in the vehicle, including PVA, water, and ethylene glycol, to obtain an ink formulation. The ink has the zeta potential of −24.8 ± 4.2 mV at pH = 7.2, and the average particle size is about 500 nm and 90 nm, corresponding to the length and width of rod-shaped pigments. The fluorescent ink has a bright yellow emission at 565 nm with the respective CIE (0.42, 0.46) coordinates under UV irradiation 365 nm. The ZnO nanoparticles-based fluorescent ink is environmentally friendly and can be used in the fields of information encryption and anti-counterfeiting packaging. In further investigation, we will improve the PL intensity of ZnO nanoparticles and modify the ink formulation to suit inkjet printing and flexographic inks.
Thanh Phuong Nguyen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work belongs to the project grant No: T2024-212. funded by Ho Chi Minh City University of Technology and Education, Vietnam.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The emergence of counterfeit trade: a literature review
Eur. J. Market.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Staake, T., The emergence of counterfeit trade: A literature review. European Journal of Marketing, 43(3/4) (2009) 320-349.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: New insights into unethical counterfeit consumption
J. Bus. Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bian, X., Wang, K.-Y., Smith, A., & Yannopoulou, N., New insights into unethical counterfeit consumption. Journal of Business Research, 69 (10) (2016) 4249-4258.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent cellulose-based materials for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting
Coord. Chem. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zongyan Quan, Qi Zhang, Hongjuan Li, Shiguo Sun, Yongqian Xu, Fluorescent cellulose-based materials for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting, Coordination Chemistry Reviews 493 (2023) 215287.
